nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical and subtropical coastal marine environments; rocky substrate, reef flats, and sandy areas in the photic zone
nativeRange.biotope: Marine benthic; anchors via rhizoids into rock, rubble, or sand substrate

imageCandidates.notes: Mosaic Macros product image — sole hobby trade source for this species. Shows upright fuzzy cylindrical blade form.

summary: A rare specialty Caulerpa with upright, fuzzy, cylindrical fronds resembling cat tails. Slower-growing than most Caulerpa relatives; suited for display tanks. Shares genus-level allelopathy and crash risk.
careNotes:
Cat Tail Caulerpa (Caulerpa lanuginosa) is a siphonous green macroalgae distinguished by its upright, fuzzy, cylindrical blades — a distinctly textured form that sets it apart from the flat blades of C. prolifera or the grape-cluster uprights of C. racemosa. It is considered rarer in the hobby than most other Caulerpa, and Mosaic Macros is currently the primary (and likely sole) hobby trade source.

GROWTH RATE: Unlike most Caulerpa species commonly described as fast-growing, Mosaic Macros explicitly notes that C. lanuginosa is 'fairly slow growing.' This has practical implications: nutrient-export capacity is lower than fast-growing relatives; the colony should not be harvested as aggressively; and establishing a new cutting may take longer than with C. prolifera or C. racemosa.

ALLELOPATHY: As with all Caulerpa, this species is considered capable of releasing secondary metabolites — including caulerpin and caulerpenyne — into the water column. These compounds have been documented to inhibit coral growth and cause stress in sensitive invertebrates. Physical separation from SPS corals is generally advisable; activated carbon use in the system is commonly recommended as a precaution.

GOING SEXUAL / REPRODUCTIVE CRASH: Caulerpa species can undergo holocarpy — a mass reproductive event in which the entire organism converts its cytoplasm to gametes, effectively dying and releasing a large pulse of organic matter into the tank. Triggers include sharp nutrient drops (running nitrate/phosphate to near zero), disrupted photoperiod, or rapid temperature swings. Mitigation strategies include: maintaining a continuous 24-hour lighting schedule in refugium settings; harvesting regularly to keep the colony in active vegetative growth; keeping nutrients at stable low-to-moderate levels (not zero); and removing any specimen immediately upon detecting reproductive structures (white or translucent fronds, milky water discharge).

INVASIVE CONCERN: All Caulerpa species are considered potentially invasive in non-native marine environments. Captive specimens must never be released into natural waterways. Sale is banned in California; Mosaic Macros cannot ship to that state.

POST-SHIPMENT APPEARANCE: Freshly arrived specimens commonly appear white or translucent due to chloroplast displacement during transit darkness. This is normal and typically reverses within hours of light exposure.

CROSS-REFERENCE: See also caulerpa-prolifera (sw-algae-004, blade-form), sea-grapes (sw-algae-016, C. racemosa, grape-cluster form), and cactus-caulerpa (sw-algae-025, C. cupressoides, cactus-branch form) for closely related Caulerpa siblings sharing these allelopathy, crash, and invasive caveats.
breedingNotes: Propagates via fragmentation and vegetative tip growth along the stolon, consistent with the Caulerpa genus. Due to its slow growth rate relative to other Caulerpa, re-establishment of cuttings may take longer than keepers familiar with faster relatives would expect. Sexual reproduction (holocarpy) is a destructive stress-triggered event and not a managed propagation method; see careNotes for prevention strategies.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole hobby trade source identified for this species. Confirms scientific name Caulerpa lanuginosa, fuzzy cylindrical blade morphology, slow growth rate relative to other Caulerpa, invasive/California-ban warning, and standard Caulerpa post-shipment appearance note.

additional.name: Caulerpa prolifera (sibling species, cross-reference)
additional.notes: Sibling Caulerpa (sw-algae-004). Water parameters, allelopathy documentation, crash behavior, and invasive caveats cross-referenced from the researched prolifera entry. Family Caulerpaceae, Order Bryopsidales confirmed consistent across genus.
